subject: Statistics
title: The Bayesian Brain
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on The Bayesian Brain.

hook.text: Your brain doesn't see the world directly. It predicts what it expects, then corrects with sensory input.

buildup.text: Neuroscience suggests the brain is a Bayesian prediction machine, constantly updating its model of reality.

discovery.text: Optical illusions work because the brain's prior is so strong it overrides the actual sensory evidence.

twist.text: Anxiety may be a prior gone wrong — the brain predicting danger when none exists, and resisting evidence of safety.

climax.text: If the brain is Bayesian, then Bayes' theorem isn't just a formula. It's a description of how we think.

punchline.text: Your brain was Bayesian before Bayes was born.

quiz.question: What does the 'Bayesian brain' hypothesis propose?
options: The brain constantly predicts what it expects and updates those predictions with sensory input
The brain uses only raw sensory data with no predictions
Bayesian math was designed to model brain cells
